name: The Human Heart
description: How the heart is built and how blood moves through it: the four chambers, the four valves, the great vessels, the conduction system, and the full path of circulation as a step-by-step sequence.

fields.front: Which heart chamber receives deoxygenated blood returning from the body?
fields.extra: It fills from the superior and inferior vena cava, then passes blood to the right ventricle below it.

fields.front: Which chamber pumps deoxygenated blood toward the lungs?
fields.extra: It ejects blood through the pulmonary valve into the pulmonary arteries.

fields.front: Which chamber receives oxygen-rich blood returning from the lungs?
fields.extra: It fills from the four pulmonary veins.

fields.front: Which chamber pumps oxygen-rich blood out to the whole body?
fields.extra: It has the thickest, most muscular wall of the four chambers.

fields.front: Which chamber has the thickest wall, and why?
fields.back: The left ventricle, because it must push blood to the entire body against high pressure
fields.extra: The right ventricle is thinner because the lungs are a low-pressure circuit right next door.

fields.front: Which valve lies between the right atrium and the right ventricle?
fields.extra: An atrioventricular (AV) valve with three flaps. Memory aid: try (tricuspid) before you buy (bicuspid), so tricuspid is on the right.

fields.front: Which valve guards the exit from the right ventricle toward the lungs?
fields.extra: A semilunar (half-moon) valve between the right ventricle and the pulmonary artery.

fields.front: Which valve lies between the left atrium and the left ventricle?
fields.extra: The only AV valve with two flaps; named for its likeness to a bishop's mitre.

fields.front: Which valve guards the exit from the left ventricle into the aorta?
fields.extra: A semilunar valve; the coronary arteries branch off just above it.

fields.front: What are the two AV valves and the two semilunar valves?
fields.back: AV valves: tricuspid and mitral. Semilunar valves: pulmonary and aortic
fields.extra: AV valves sit between atria and ventricles; semilunar valves guard the arteries leaving the heart.

fields.front: What makes the 'lub-dub' sound of a heartbeat?
fields.back: 'Lub' is the two AV valves snapping shut; 'dub' is the two semilunar valves snapping shut
fields.extra: The sound is the valves closing, not blood or muscle moving.

fields.front: Which vessels return deoxygenated blood from the body to the heart?
fields.back: The superior and inferior vena cava
fields.extra: They empty into the right atrium; they are the body's largest veins.

fields.front: What is unusual about the blood in the pulmonary artery?
fields.back: It is the only artery that carries deoxygenated blood
fields.extra: Arteries are defined by carrying blood away from the heart, not by being oxygen-rich. This one runs from the right ventricle to the lungs.

fields.front: What is unusual about the blood in the pulmonary veins?
fields.back: They are the only veins that carry oxygen-rich blood
fields.extra: Four of them return freshly oxygenated blood from the lungs to the left atrium.

fields.front: What is the largest artery in the body?
fields.extra: It carries oxygen-rich blood from the left ventricle and arches over the heart to supply the whole body.

fields.front: Which side of the heart drives the pulmonary circulation, to and from the lungs?
fields.extra: A short, low-pressure loop: right heart to lungs and back.

fields.front: Which side of the heart drives the systemic circulation, to and from the body?
fields.extra: A long, high-pressure loop: left heart to the whole body and back.

fields.front: What is the muscular wall that divides the left and right sides of the heart called?
fields.extra: The interatrial septum divides the atria; the interventricular septum divides the ventricles. A hole in it is a 'hole in the heart'.

fields.front: What is the thick muscular middle layer of the heart wall called?
fields.extra: Cardiac muscle, sandwiched between the inner lining (endocardium) and outer layer (epicardium). It never tires.

fields.front: What is the tough double-walled sac that surrounds the heart?
fields.extra: It anchors the heart and holds a little fluid so it can beat without friction.

fields.front: What do the coronary arteries supply?
fields.back: The heart muscle's own blood supply
fields.extra: They branch from the aorta just above the aortic valve. A blockage starves the muscle and causes a heart attack.

fields.front: What is the heart's natural pacemaker?
fields.extra: A patch of cells in the right atrium wall that fires the electrical signal setting the rhythm, about 60 to 100 beats per minute at rest.

fields.front: What relays the electrical signal from the atria to the ventricles?
fields.extra: It briefly delays the signal so the atria finish emptying before the ventricles contract.

fields.front: Roughly how big is the human heart?
fields.back: About the size of a clenched fist
fields.extra: It weighs around 250 to 350 grams in an adult.

fields.front: About how many times does a human heart beat over an average lifetime?
fields.extra: At about 100,000 beats a day, it never rests.

fields.title: Trace a drop of blood through the heart and lungs, starting from the body
